name: reqwest
what_it_does: The most popular async HTTP client for Rust. Built on Hyper and Tokio with a high-level ergonomic API. Supports JSON serialization/deserialization via Serde, cookie management, proxies, redirect following, TLS (rustls or native-tls), and streaming responses. The go-to choice for making HTTP requests in Rust agents and services.
use_cases: Make HTTP API calls to LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) from Rust agent code with async/await and JSON body handling
Build Rust agent tools that call REST APIs with automatic JSON deserialization into typed Serde structs
Implement streaming response consumption for LLM token streaming with reqwest's async byte stream API
Make authenticated HTTP requests with bearer token injection via request builder middleware
Build Rust-based web scrapers or data fetchers for agent pipelines with connection pooling and retry logic
not_for: Blocking/synchronous Rust code — use reqwest's blocking feature for sync contexts but prefer async
WebSocket connections — reqwest is HTTP-only; use tokio-tungstenite for WebSocket
Extremely high performance raw HTTP needs — hyper directly provides lower-level control with less overhead
best_when: You need to make HTTP requests in async Rust code — reqwest is the standard ergonomic choice with batteries included.
avoid_when: You need WebSocket support or the lowest-level HTTP control — use tokio-tungstenite for WS or hyper directly for raw HTTP.

auth.notes: HTTP client library — auth headers/tokens are set by the caller via request builder or header middleware.

pricing.notes: MIT/Apache-2.0 dual license. Maintained by Sean McArthur.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local HTTP client. Performance limited by network and connection pool size.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: reqwest errors include error kind (network, timeout, status, decode) with source chaining. HTTP status errors include the response body for debugging. Timeout errors clearly indicate which phase timed out.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GET/HEAD requests are idempotent by HTTP spec. POST/PUT/PATCH require application-level idempotency. reqwest does not add idempotency keys automatically.
known_agent_gotchas: reqwest builds a connection pool per Client instance — create one Client and reuse it; creating a new Client per request leaks connections and is slow
Response bodies must be explicitly consumed — drop a Response without reading the body and the connection may not return to the pool properly
The blocking feature (reqwest::blocking) cannot be used from within a Tokio runtime (deadlock) — use the async API or spawn_blocking for sync code in async contexts
TLS backend selection (rustls vs native-tls) is a compile-time feature — choose one and stick with it; mixing causes linker errors
Redirect following is enabled by default (up to 10 redirects) — use redirect(Policy::none()) to disable for APIs that use 3xx for semantic responses
JSON deserialization errors from response.json::<T>() lose the original response body — capture response.text() first for debugging when deserialization fails

security_score_components.security_notes: Rust memory safety prevents buffer overflows. TLS enabled by default (rustls or native-tls). Certificate verification on by default — disable only for development. No ambient credential storage.
